title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Kenai canoe trails among best AUTUMN: Lake systems offer quiet wilderness and chance for fresh trout meal. By TONY LEWIS Special to the Anchorage Daily News Published: October 9, 2005 Last Modified: October 14, 2005 at 10:27 AM Click to enlarge Gavia Lake won't take your breath away. You might see a few loons on the water or a moose on the bank if you're lucky. In Southcentral Alaska, where snow-peaked fjords and salmon-choked rivers compete for attention, Gavia is just one of hundreds of lakes that speckle the low-lying spruce forests of the western Kenai Peninsula. Not much here to attract professional photographers. Every year, though, more than 10,000 visitors paddle Gavia and the other 69 or so lakes that make up a system of canoe trails in the Kenai National Wildlife Refuge. "It is n…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Animal rights group baits anglers with ethics pledge PETA asks state to make fishermen take mandatory oath to ease cruelty By DOUG O'HARRA Anchorage Daily News Published: October 28, 2005 Last Modified: October 28, 2005 at 07:19 AM The animal rights group PETA wants fishermen to promise they will follow a federal "Code of Angling Ethics" before they can legally hook salmon, trout and halibut in Alaska. Last week, People for the Ethical Treatment of Animals faxed a letter to Mc- Kie Campbell, commissioner of the Department of Fish and Game, asking him to require Alaska anglers to take an angling ethics pledge before they can buy a fishing license. The department hasn't officially responded to PETA, and Campbell was out of state and unavailable for comment Thursday. But a state fisheries off…

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Craig Medred: Don't let one mistake on water cascade into more (10-23-2005) CRAIG MEDRED Outdoors Published: October 23, 2005 Last Modified: December 20, 2005 at 06:20 AM SKILAK LAKE -- Less than a mile from the beach with the bow of the Klepper kayak plunging into the front of the waves and darkness closing in, the decision to gamble on a late-night crossing of this notoriously wind-tossed lake looked frighteningly bad. Just tens of minutes before, the night had offered such promise. Winds that had been blowing strong out of the east for two days appeared to be easing. Daylight was, admittedly, fading fast, but a bright moon was rising. So we broke camp, slammed gear as fast as possible into the two-man kayak, slipped into dry suits and personal flotation devices, grabbed the paddles and …

title: Anchorage Daily News: Alaska's Newspaper
summary: Middle-aged mushers learn danger is only a blink away CRAIG MEDRED OUTDOORS Published: April 9, 2006 Last Modified: April 9, 2006 at 03:09 AM Back home in Minnesota now, the eye that musher Paul Ellering froze in Alaska last month is slowly healing. The pain is gone, the vision apparently returning. This week he was to try putting a contact lens in place for the first time in weeks. The last time he used a contact, he couldn't get it out. "The lens froze to the eye,'' Ellering said. He said this matter-of-factly over the telephone from his home. A retired professional wrestler, Ellering had to chuckle at the irony of spending his career in an occupation where participants sometimes stick their fingers in each other's eyes only to suffer his most serious eye injury in the Iditarod Trail Sle…
